methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series G → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.25x) to $800.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 22 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 59.8x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
SpaceX, a Series G space and aerospace company, has raised over $10 billion to date and carries a last post-money valuation of $800 billion. The Space & Aerospace sector includes 5 public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and 8 private peers including Rocket Lab, Blue Origin, and Planet Labs, though the company's scale places it in a narrow peer set.

Of three valuation methodologies evaluated, only stage-progression proved applicable. This method applies a typical Series G step-up multiplier of 1.25x to the last post-money valuation of $800 billion, implying a value of $1.0 trillion. Peer-median analysis was not viable due to insufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comparables within the narrow Space & Aerospace tier (fewer than three datapoints met filtering criteria). Public-comp anchoring, while the sector's P/S median stands at 59.8x across 6 public tickers, could not be executed because the target's revenue is not disclosed.

Given the methodological constraints, the analysis supports a triangulated range of $850 billion (low) to $1.1 trillion (high), with a median of $1.0 trillion. Key uncertainties include the applicability of standard stage-progression multiples to frontier-tech companies at this valuation scale and the absence of disclosed revenue data to cross-check against public multiples. The narrow peer set and lack of recent comparable late-stage transactions in this sector further limit conviction.
